facts.text: Gouverneur was founded in 1805 and grew around the Oswegatchie River, which interestingly flows in two directions through the town.
The town’s name honors Gouverneur Morris, a prominent statesman and landowner from the early republic.
In 1850 the community of Gouverneur separated from the town and incorporated as a village, marking a key moment in its local governance.
The United States Post Office in Gouverneur earned a place on the National Register of Historic Places in 1989, a testament to its historic architecture.
Historically, Gouverneur’s economy thrived on mining marble, talc, and zinc, resources that helped shape the town’s early industrial character.
Three major routes—US‑11, NY‑58, and NY‑812—converge in the village, a junction that has long served as a regional crossroads.
